input: Prof. Smith works at a large university. At this university, in order to get new computers from the university, faculty like Prof. Smith must send an application to two administrative committees, the IT committee and the department budget committee. Prof. Smith will be able to get her new computers if the IT committee approves her application or the department budget committee approves her application. Only one of the committees needs to approve her application for her to get the new computers. The IT committee almost always approves these applications. The department budget committee also almost always approves these applications. The budget committee is notorious for approving almost every application they receive. Prof. Smith sends in her applications. Each committee meets independently and they decide without talking to each other, but their meetings are scheduled for the exact same time. The IT committee approves her application, and as expected, the department budget committee approves her application. So, Prof. Smith got her new computers. Does the department budget committee cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application?
debate_transcript: [Causal_Scientist]: Prof. Smith works at a large university. At this university, in order to get new computers from the university, faculty like Prof. Smith must send an application to two administrative committees, the IT committee and the department budget committee. Prof. Smith will be able to get her new computers if the IT committee approves her application or the department budget committee approves her application. Only one of the committees needs to approve her application for her to get the new computers. The IT committee almost always approves these applications. The department budget committee also almost always approves these applications. The budget committee is notorious for approving almost every application they receive. Prof. Smith sends in her applications. Each committee meets independently and they decide without talking to each other, but their meetings are scheduled for the exact same time. The IT committee approves her application, and as expected, the department budget committee approves her application. So, Prof. Smith got her new computers. Does the department budget committee cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: This is an interesting question that touches on the nature of causation and the role of multiple sufficient causes. To determine if the department budget committee caused the approval of Prof. Smith's application, we need to consider the concept of 'INUS' conditions—insufficient but non-redundant parts of a condition which is itself unnecessary but sufficient for the occurrence of the effect. In this case, both committees are individually sufficient to cause the approval, but neither is necessary. The IT committee alone could have caused the approval, and the department budget committee alone could have caused the approval. So, the question becomes: can we attribute causation to the department budget committee when the IT committee also approved the application? This seems to be a case of preemption, where one cause (the IT committee's approval) preempts the other (the department budget committee's approval). However, if we consider the counterfactual—what would have happened if the department budget committee had not approved the application—the approval would still have occurred due to the IT committee's approval. This suggests that the department budget committee's approval did not cause the outcome, as the outcome would have been the same without it. But this is a complex issue, and I'm curious to hear others' perspectives.

[Legal_Expert]: From a legal perspective, we often look at causation in terms of 'but-for' tests. In this case, the 'but-for' test would suggest that the department budget committee's approval was not the cause, because the approval would have happened 'but-for' their approval—due to the IT committee's approval. However, this is a simplistic view. In some jurisdictions, we consider 'joint causation' or 'concurrent causation,' where multiple factors contribute to an outcome. If we apply this lens, both committees contributed to the approval, even if one's contribution was redundant. The question then becomes whether the university's policy intended for both committees to have a role in the approval process, and if so, the department budget committee's approval could be seen as part of the causal chain. It's a nuanced issue, and I'm interested in how others interpret this scenario.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I'm not sure I understand all the big words being thrown around here, but from my perspective, it seems like both committees played a role in getting Prof. Smith her new computers. Even if the IT committee alone could have approved it, the fact that the department budget committee also approved it means that they were part of the process. It's like if two people are holding up a heavy box—even if one person could hold it up alone, the other person is still helping, right? So, I think the department budget committee did cause the approval, even if it was a bit redundant.

[Causal_Scientist]: From a causal perspective, we need to examine the counterfactual dependencies to determine causation. The department budget committee's approval did not cause the outcome because the outcome would have occurred regardless of their decision—this is known as preemption. The IT committee's approval alone was sufficient to ensure Prof. Smith got her new computers. However, this doesn't mean the department budget committee's approval had no role in the causal structure. It's an instance of redundant causation, where multiple sufficient causes exist. The counterfactual test reveals that the department budget committee's approval did not change the outcome, but it's important to note that causation is not always binary. The department budget committee's approval was part of the causal web, even if it was not necessary for the outcome. This aligns with the legal concept of joint causation mentioned earlier. The question then becomes whether we want to attribute causation based on necessity, sufficiency, or some combination of both. What are your thoughts on this, Ethical_Philosopher? Do you think the counterfactual test is the most appropriate way to assess causation in this scenario, or are there other frameworks we should consider?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: This is a fascinating discussion that bridges legal, scientific, and everyday perspectives on causation. As an ethical philosopher, I'm particularly interested in the intentionality and moral responsibility aspects of this scenario. From an ethical standpoint, we might consider whether the department budget committee's approval carries any moral weight, even if it was redundant. If the committee's role is merely procedural and their approval doesn't change the outcome, does it still hold any ethical significance? For instance, if the committee's approval was a mere formality, it might not carry much moral weight. However, if the committee's approval was the result of a deliberative process where they considered the merits of the application, it might carry more ethical significance, even if it was redundant. This raises questions about the nature of moral responsibility in cases of overdetermination. Additionally, we might consider the ethical implications of the university's policy that requires applications to go through both committees. Is this policy justifiable, or does it create unnecessary redundancy? From a virtue ethics perspective, we might ask whether the committees are acting with wisdom and due diligence, or if they are merely going through the motions. These questions might not directly answer the causation question, but they add another layer to the discussion that I find intriguing. What are your thoughts on the ethical dimensions of this scenario, and how they might intersect with the causation debate?

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I see where you're both coming from, but I'm still trying to wrap my head around this. From my everyday perspective, it seems like the department budget committee did something— they approved the application, right? Even if the IT committee would have done it alone, the other committee still did their part. It's like if two people promise to help you move, and you can move all your stuff with just one of them, but the other still shows up and helps. You'd still thank both of them, right? Now, about the ethical part—if the committee just rubber-stamps everything, that doesn't seem right. They should actually think about the applications, even if the IT committee is also looking at them. It's like having two locks on your door—both might not be necessary, but it's good to have that extra security. So, I think both committees should do their jobs properly, even if one of them alone could get the job done.

[Legal_Expert]: From a legal standpoint, the ethical dimensions you've raised are quite relevant, particularly when considering negligence and duty of care. If the department budget committee is merely going through the motions without proper deliberation, they might be failing in their duty of care. This could potentially lead to liability issues if their negligence results in harm— for instance, if they approved an application that should have been denied due to budget constraints, and that denial could have prevented a financial crisis. In terms of causation, if we consider the committees' actions as part of a larger procedural duty, their approvals could be seen as contributing factors, even if redundant. This aligns with the idea of concurrent causation in legal theory. However, proving negligence or liability in such cases can be challenging, as it requires demonstrating that the committee's failure to properly exercise their duty directly resulted in harm. It's a complex interplay of procedural expectations, ethical responsibilities, and legal liabilities. I'm curious to hear more about how these aspects might influence the broader understanding of causation in this scenario.

[Causal_Scientist]: The ethical and legal perspectives you've raised add valuable depth to this discussion. From a causal science standpoint, we can explore the concept of 'causal contribution' beyond the binary notion of necessity. The department budget committee's approval, even if redundant, can be seen as a contributing cause. This aligns with the legal concept of concurrent causation and the ethical idea of shared responsibility. To assess this, we might consider the following: if we were to intervene on the department budget committee's approval (for example, if they had denied the application), would that have changed the outcome? In this case, the outcome would have remained the same due to the IT committee's approval. However, this doesn't negate the committee's role in the causal structure. It's akin to having multiple switches that can independently turn on a light—flipping one switch doesn't negate the causal role of the other switches. This perspective allows us to attribute causal contribution to the department budget committee, even if their approval was not necessary for the outcome. Ethical_Philosopher, how does this notion of causal contribution align with your thoughts on moral responsibility in cases of overdetermination? And Legal_Expert, does this perspective on concurrent causation resonate with your understanding of liability in such scenarios?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: The notion of causal contribution you've presented, Causal_Scientist, resonates with the ethical theory of shared moral responsibility. In cases of overdetermination, where multiple sufficient causes exist, we can still attribute moral responsibility to each cause based on their contribution to the outcome. This aligns with the idea of 'causal contribution' you've described. From an ethical standpoint, the department budget committee's approval can be seen as a contributing factor to the outcome, even if it was redundant. This is because their approval was part of the process that led to the outcome, and they had a role in ensuring that the outcome occurred. However, the moral weight of their approval might be less than that of the IT committee's approval, as the IT committee's approval alone was sufficient to ensure the outcome. This raises questions about the distribution of moral responsibility in cases of overdetermination. Additionally, the ethical dimensions of the university's policy come into play. If the policy requires applications to go through both committees, then both committees share moral responsibility for the outcome, even if one's approval was redundant. This is because they are both part of the process that the university has established to ensure that applications are approved. From a virtue ethics perspective, we might also consider whether the committees are acting with wisdom and due diligence. If the department budget committee is merely going through the motions without proper deliberation, they might be failing in their duty of care, as Legal_Expert mentioned. This could potentially lead to liability issues if their negligence results in harm. So, the ethical dimensions of this scenario are complex and multifaceted, and they intersect with the legal and causal science perspectives in interesting ways.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I think I'm starting to understand this better. So, even if the department budget committee's approval wasn't strictly necessary, they still played a part in the process. It's like when you're baking a cake, and you have two eggs, but you only need one to make it work. The second egg is still contributing to the cake, even if it's not essential. Now, about the moral responsibility part—if the committee is just approving everything without thinking, that's not right. They should do their job properly, even if the other committee is also checking things. It's like having two parents checking their kid's homework—even if one parent could do it alone, both should still care and look at it. So, I think both committees should take their jobs seriously, even if one of them alone could get the job done. But I'm still not sure how this all fits together. Can someone explain it in simpler terms?

[Causal_Scientist]: Let me try to simplify this. Imagine you have two keys that can open the same door. If you use one key to open the door, the other key didn't cause the door to open, but it could have. In this scenario, the department budget committee is like the second key. Their approval didn't cause the outcome because the IT committee's approval alone was sufficient. However, the department budget committee's approval was still a potential cause—it had the capacity to cause the outcome if the IT committee had not approved the application. This is what we call a 'potential cause' or 'back-up cause' in causal science. It's similar to how the second egg in your cake analogy contributes to the final product, even if it's not strictly necessary. The ethical dimension comes into play when we consider the responsibilities of the committees. Even if their approval is not strictly necessary, they have a duty to properly evaluate the applications. This is like the two parents checking homework—both should take their roles seriously, even if one could handle it alone. So, while the department budget committee's approval didn't cause the outcome in this specific instance, it was still part of the causal structure and carries ethical responsibilities. Does that help clarify things, Ordinary_Citizen?

[Legal_Expert]: To put it in legal terms, think of it this way: if the department budget committee is like a backup security guard. Even if the main security guard can handle the situation alone, the backup is still part of the security system. If something goes wrong, both guards could potentially share liability, even if one was sufficient. In this case, the department budget committee's approval didn't 'cause' the outcome in the strictest legal sense, but it was part of the process that led to the outcome. This is similar to the concept of concurrent causation, where multiple factors contribute to an event, and each can be held partially responsible. From a negligence perspective, if the department budget committee failed to properly evaluate the application, they could be found liable for any resulting harm, even if their approval wasn't strictly necessary. So, while their approval didn't 'cause' the outcome, it was still a contributing factor in the legal sense. This aligns with the causal science and ethical perspectives you've discussed. It's a complex interplay, but the key takeaway is that all parties involved share some degree of responsibility, even if their individual contributions were redundant.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: Okay, I think I get it now. So, the department budget committee is like a backup that didn't have to do anything this time, but they're still part of the system. They should still do their job properly, just in case they're needed. It's like having a spare tire in your car—you might not need it, but it's important to have it and make sure it's in good condition. So, even if they didn't cause the approval this time, they're still responsible for doing their part. Thanks for explaining it in simpler terms! I think I can see why all this matters now.
